q: What is true about the magnitude of velocity in uniform circular motion?[cite: 1]
Why is an object revolving in a circle continuously accelerating even if the speed is constant?[cite: 1]
a: Because the mass is constantly shifting
Because the direction of the velocity continuously changes

q: Where does the centripetal acceleration vector point in uniform circular motion?[cite: 1]
a: Towards the centre of the circular path

q: What is the mathematical relationship between centripetal acceleration $a_c$, linear speed $v$, and radius $r$?[cite: 1]
What is the angle between the centripetal acceleration vector and the linear velocity vector in uniform circular motion?[cite: 1]
What causes tangential acceleration in nonuniform circular motion?[cite: 1]
What is the resultant acceleration in nonuniform circular motion composed of?[cite: 1]
a: The difference between angular and linear accelerations
The vector sum of centripetal and tangential accelerations
